title: Human impact on ecosystems
examTip: Human impact questions often ask you to explain causes AND consequences — don't just say 'pollution is bad'. AQA wants mechanisms: why does excess fertiliser cause eutrophication, what actually happens step by step. Also know the difference between different types of pollution and their specific effects.
introTips: Human impact on ecosystems is a big topic — but AQA focuses on a few key processes: eutrophication, deforestation, and pollution types.
Eutrophication is a favourite extended answer target. Learn the full sequence: fertiliser runoff → algal bloom → decomposers → oxygen depletion → fish die.
For each human impact, know the cause, the mechanism of harm, and the consequence.
intro: Humans are now the dominant force shaping ecosystems on Earth. We clear forests, pollute waterways, introduce non-native species, and change the climate. Understanding the mechanisms behind these impacts — not just listing them — is what AQA rewards in extended writing questions.

keyPoints.heading: Pollution and its effects
keyPoints.content: Human activities release pollutants that harm ecosystems. Key types: Water pollution — untreated sewage, industrial chemicals, and agricultural runoff (fertilisers, pesticides) contaminate rivers and lakes. Air pollution — burning fossil fuels releases sulfur dioxide (causes acid rain), nitrogen oxides, and particulates. Acid rain damages plants and acidifies lakes, killing aquatic organisms. Land pollution — landfill sites, chemical spills, and plastic waste accumulate in soil. Pesticide bioaccumulation: pesticides that don't break down (e.g. DDT) accumulate in body fat. Predators at the top of the food chain get the highest concentrations — this is called biomagnification.
writeBullets: Water pollution: sewage, industrial chemicals, fertiliser/pesticide runoff
Air pollution: fossil fuels → SO₂ (acid rain), nitrogen oxides, particulates
Acid rain: damages plants, acidifies lakes → kills aquatic organisms
Pesticide bioaccumulation (biomagnification): concentration increases up food chain
Plastic pollution: accumulates in marine food chains, physically harms organisms

keyTerms.def: Rain made acidic by dissolved sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxides from burning fossil fuels — damages plants and aquatic ecosystems
The increasing concentration of a pollutant (e.g. a pesticide) at each trophic level as it accumulates in body fat — highest concentrations at the top of the food chain

keyPoints.memTip: Biomagnification: pesticide → producers absorb small amount → primary consumers eat many producers → secondary consumers eat many primary consumers → concentration multiplies at each level. Top predators get the highest dose.

keyPoints.heading: Eutrophication — the nitrate problem
keyPoints.content: Eutrophication is a sequence of events triggered by excess nutrients (mainly nitrates and phosphates) entering a waterway, usually from agricultural fertiliser runoff or sewage. The full sequence: (1) excess nitrates and phosphates drain from fields into rivers/lakes; (2) algae and plants use the nutrients and grow rapidly — forming an algal bloom that covers the water surface; (3) the algal bloom blocks sunlight, so underwater plants cannot photosynthesise and die; (4) decomposers (bacteria) decompose the dead plants and algae, respiring rapidly; (5) this rapid bacterial respiration uses up the dissolved oxygen in the water; (6) without oxygen, fish and other aquatic organisms suffocate and die — the water becomes deoxygenated.
writeBullets: 1. Nitrate/phosphate fertilisers leach from fields into waterways
2. Algae and aquatic plants grow rapidly → algal bloom covers water surface
3. Algal bloom blocks sunlight → underwater plants can't photosynthesise → die
4. Dead organic matter decomposed by bacteria
5. Bacteria respire rapidly → use up dissolved oxygen
6. Fish and other aquatic organisms cannot get enough oxygen → die (deoxygenation)

keyTerms.def: The process by which excess nutrients (nitrates/phosphates) cause rapid algal growth, leading to oxygen depletion and death of aquatic organisms
Rapid, excessive growth of algae on the surface of a waterway, triggered by high nutrient levels
The removal of dissolved oxygen from water by rapidly respiring bacteria — kills aerobic organisms such as fish

keyPoints.memTip: Eutrophication in 6 steps: Fertiliser runoff → Algal bloom → Blocks sunlight → Underwater plants die → Decomposers respire → Oxygen depleted → Fish die. Learn this sequence in order — AQA often asks you to describe or explain each step.

keyPoints.heading: Deforestation and land use change
keyPoints.content: Large-scale deforestation destroys biodiversity directly and disrupts the carbon and water cycles. Effects: (1) loss of habitat → species extinction; (2) increased atmospheric CO₂ (trees no longer absorb it; burning/decomposing trees releases stored carbon); (3) reduced transpiration → less water vapour returned to atmosphere → reduced rainfall in the area; (4) soil erosion — tree roots hold soil; without trees, topsoil washes away in rain. Other land use changes: intensive farming uses pesticides and herbicides that reduce biodiversity; monocultures (one crop species) create large areas of low biodiversity vulnerable to disease. Overgrazing by livestock destroys vegetation and compacts soil.
writeBullets: Deforestation: loss of habitat → species extinction
CO₂ increases: trees don't absorb CO₂; burning/decomposing wood releases stored carbon
Reduced transpiration → less rainfall in the deforested area
Soil erosion: no tree roots to hold soil → topsoil washes away
Monocultures: low biodiversity, vulnerable to disease

keyTerms.def: The large-scale removal of trees, causing habitat loss, increased CO₂, reduced transpiration, and soil erosion
Farming a single crop species across a large area — reduces biodiversity and increases vulnerability to disease
The washing or blowing away of topsoil, often following deforestation when tree roots no longer hold the soil in place

keyPoints.memTip: Deforestation effects: HCTE — Habitat loss, CO₂ increase, Transpiration decrease (less rainfall), Erosion. Four consequences from one action. AQA often asks you to explain the CO₂ increase in detail — burning + decomposition + no more photosynthesis.

tables.title: Eutrophication — step by step
rows: Dense algal bloom on surface acts as a barrier
Decomposers respire rapidly on dead matter
Large amount of organic matter to decompose
Bacterial respiration consumes all available O₂
Cannot get sufficient oxygen from the deoxygenated water

0.question: In eutrophication, why do fish die?
options: They are poisoned directly by the fertiliser chemicals
Decomposing bacteria use up all the dissolved oxygen, leaving none for the fish
The algal bloom produces toxins that kill aquatic organisms
Increased algal growth removes all the food the fish rely on
0.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — it's the bacteria, not the algae or fertiliser directly, that cause fish death. Bacteria rapidly decompose the dead plant matter and use all the oxygen.
0.wrongFeedback: Fish die because decomposing bacteria respire rapidly as they break down the dead plant material, using up all the dissolved oxygen in the water. Without dissolved oxygen, fish and other aerobic organisms suffocate. The fertiliser itself doesn't directly kill fish — the chain of events does.

1.question: Which of the following is a direct consequence of large-scale deforestation?
options: Increased rainfall in the deforested area
Increased atmospheric CO₂ due to loss of photosynthesis and release of stored carbon
Reduced soil erosion because vegetation is removed
Increased species biodiversity due to more open land
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — deforestation reduces photosynthesis (less CO₂ absorbed) and releases stored carbon (burning/decomposition), both increasing atmospheric CO₂.
1.wrongFeedback: Deforestation increases atmospheric CO₂ because: (1) trees are no longer photosynthesising and absorbing CO₂, and (2) when trees are burned or decompose, the carbon stored in their tissue is released as CO₂. Rainfall actually DECREASES (less transpiration), soil erosion INCREASES (no roots to hold soil), and biodiversity DECREASES (habitat lost).

commonMistakes: In eutrophication, saying 'the fertiliser kills the fish' — the fertiliser is the trigger, but fish die because bacteria use up dissolved oxygen. The mechanism matters.
Stopping the eutrophication explanation at 'algal bloom' — the algal bloom is just step 2 of 6. Continue to: blocks sunlight → plants die → bacteria decompose → O₂ depleted → fish die.
Saying deforestation increases rainfall — it's the opposite. Trees transpire water into the atmosphere; without trees, less water vapour = less rainfall in that area.
Saying pesticides directly kill top predators — biomagnification is the mechanism. Small amounts are eaten at each level, accumulating in body fat, until top predators get a toxic dose.
examTips: Eutrophication is a common 4–6 mark question. Learn all 6–7 steps. Each step is typically worth a mark — don't skip any.
For deforestation questions, give at least three different consequences: biodiversity loss, CO₂ increase, reduced rainfall, soil erosion. One consequence won't get full marks on a 4-mark question.
Biomagnification questions: explain that the pollutant isn't broken down, accumulates in fat, and the concentration multiplies at each trophic level — highest at the top.
AQA sometimes gives data on oxygen levels or nitrate concentrations in a river and asks you to explain a pattern — apply eutrophication knowledge to interpret graphs.
revisionCardBullets: Water pollution: fertiliser runoff, sewage, pesticides, industrial chemicals
Acid rain: SO₂ from fossil fuels → acidifies rain → harms plants and aquatic life
Biomagnification: pollutant concentration increases up food chain — top predators most affected
Eutrophication: fertiliser → algal bloom → blocks light → plants die → bacteria decompose → O₂ depleted → fish die
Deforestation: habitat loss, CO₂ increase, less rainfall, soil erosion
Deforestation increases CO₂: less photosynthesis + burning/decomposing trees releases stored carbon
Monocultures: low biodiversity, vulnerable to a single disease
